content.text: Root cause analysis (RCA) for AI incidents is harder than for conventional infrastructure outages because the causal chain often involves non-deterministic model behaviour, multi-hop retrieval pipelines, and external provider dependencies. Standard techniques like 5 Whys work well for availability failures but can be inadequate for quality degradation, where the 'cause' is a distributional shift in model output rather than a discrete system failure. This topic covers the full toolkit: which technique to use for which incident type, and how to apply each one rigorously to an AI system.

attrs.alt: RCA technique selection matrix showing six AI incident types (latency/availability, output quality, safety, RAG, cost/quota, provider) mapped to primary techniques (5 Whys, Fishbone, Fault Tree) and supporting data sources

content.text: Why Standard RCA Techniques Need Adaptation for AI

Traditional RCA assumes a deterministic system: the same input produces the same output, so failures can be reproduced by replaying the conditions. GenAI systems violate this assumption in several ways:
Model inference is non-deterministic (temperature > 0) — exact reproduction of a bad output may be impossible
RAG pipelines introduce a retrieval step whose results depend on the state of the vector store at query time
LLM provider updates can change model behaviour silently — the model version pinned to 'gpt-4-turbo' may not be the same model it was last month
Quality degradation can be gradual and statistical — no single request fails, but the distribution of outputs shifts
Safety failures may involve adversarial inputs designed to evade detection, making the 'normal path' analysis insufficient
The adaptations below address each of these constraints.
Technique 1: 5 Whys (Availability and Performance Incidents)
The 5 Whys technique asks
'why did this happen?'

five times in succession, each answer becoming the input to the next question. The goal is to get past the proximate cause to the systemic condition. For availability failures — OOM, timeouts, pod crashes, cascade failures — 5 Whys is usually sufficient.
5 Whys Applied to an Inference Latency Incident

Symptom: Inference API P99 > 30s for 39 minutes

Why? Inference pods in us-east-1a were OOMKilled and restarting.

Why? The memory limit in the pod spec (4Gi) was below the actual
      memory requirement under production load (8Gi).

Why? A Helm values update changed the memory limit without cross-
      referencing the production memory profile.

Why? The CI pipeline validates Helm syntax but not semantic correctness
      against runtime memory profiles.

Why? When the CI pipeline was built, memory profile validation was
      considered out of scope for linting — there was no standard for it.

Root cause: Absence of semantic memory limit validation in CI.
Systemic gap: No standard exists for validating infrastructure-as-code
              changes against runtime performance profiles.
5 Whys Anti-Patterns
Anti-pattern
Example
Problem
Stopping at the proximate cause
Why? → 'OOM'. Stop.
Doesn't identify what needs to change
Attributing a why to a person
Why? → 'Alice didn't check the memory profile.'
Blameful; no systemic action follows
Jumping to solutions
Why? → 'We need better monitoring.' (not a why)
Skips the causal chain; may miss the real gap
Branching without discipline
Why 3 has four different answers explored equally
Choose the most significant branch; document others as contributing factors
Technique 2: Fishbone Diagram (Output Quality Incidents)
A fishbone diagram (Ishikawa diagram) maps all possible causes of an effect across multiple categories simultaneously. It is better suited than 5 Whys for
quality degradation
incidents where the root cause is not a single discrete failure but a combination of contributing factors across the model, prompt, data, and infrastructure dimensions.
Fishbone Categories for GenAI Quality Incidents
Category
Factors to examine
Evidence to collect
Model
Model version change, provider update, temperature/sampling params, fine-tune drift
Model version at incident time vs baseline; provider changelog
Prompt
System prompt change, few-shot examples changed, prompt length at limit, token truncation
Prompt diff between baseline and incident window; token count logs
Data / Context
RAG context quality, retrieved document freshness, embedding model version, vector store corruption
Retrieval logs; embedding model version; vector store snapshot
Infrastructure
Latency (causes premature timeout), memory pressure (context truncated), load balancer routing to degraded pod
Infra metrics at incident time; trace spans
Evaluation
Eval dataset not updated to reflect current use cases, evaluation metric mismatch
Eval scores before/during/after incident; eval dataset version
External
Training data distribution shift, provider model update, third-party API returning different data
Provider changelog; third-party API version; A/B test interference
Applying the Fishbone to a Hallucination Spike

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class FishboneAnalysis:
    """Structured fishbone for a GenAI quality incident."""
    incident_id: str
    symptom: str

    # Six categories
    model_factors: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    prompt_factors: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    data_factors: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    infra_factors: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    eval_factors: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    external_factors: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    # Identified root causes (subset of factors with confirmed causation)
    root_causes: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    contributing_factors: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

# Example: hallucination spike after silent model update
analysis = FishboneAnalysis(
    incident_id="INC-2024-04-02-007",
    symptom="Hallucination rate increased 3.2x vs baseline over 6-hour window",
    model_factors=[
        "gpt-4-turbo-preview alias silently pointed to new model checkpoint at 02:00 UTC",
        "New checkpoint has different behaviour on ambiguous factual queries",
    ],
    prompt_factors=[
        "System prompt unchanged",
        "No few-shot examples for ambiguous query pattern (not a cause but a gap)",
    ],
    data_factors=[
        "RAG context unchanged; freshness normal",
    ],
    eval_factors=[
        "Eval suite does not include ambiguous factual queries — did not catch regression",
    ],
    external_factors=[
        "OpenAI updated the gpt-4-turbo-preview alias without version notification",
    ],
    root_causes=[
        "Pinning model by alias rather than explicit version ID allows silent updates",
        "No automated hallucination regression test in deployment pipeline",
    ],
    contributing_factors=[
        "Eval suite lacks coverage of ambiguous factual query class",
        "No monitoring alert for hallucination rate deviation > 50% from baseline",
    ],
)
Technique 3: Fault Tree Analysis (Safety Incidents)
Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) starts from the
undesired top-level event
(e.g. 'user received harmful output') and works
downward

through all the conditions that had to be simultaneously true for it to occur. It uses AND gates (all sub-conditions required) and OR gates (any one sufficient). For safety incidents, FTA is more appropriate than 5 Whys because safety failures typically require multiple defence layers to fail simultaneously.
Fault Tree for a Safety Filter Bypass

TOP EVENT: User received policy-violating output
|
+-- AND gate: All three conditions were true:
    |
    +-- [A] Input bypassed the input safety filter
    |   |
    |   +-- OR gate:
    |       +-- [A1] Input was adversarially crafted to evade filter
    |       +-- [A2] Filter rule set was outdated (new attack pattern)
    |       +-- [A3] Filter was disabled for performance testing (not re-enabled)
    |
    +-- [B] LLM generated violating content
    |   |
    |   +-- OR gate:
    |       +-- [B1] Model has latent capability to generate this content
    |       +-- [B2] Few-shot examples in prompt included edge case that guided output
    |
    +-- [C] Output safety filter did not catch it
        |
        +-- OR gate:
            +-- [C1] Output filter rule did not match this form of violation
            +-- [C2] Output filter was rate-limited and sampled the output (missed)
            +-- [C3] Filter confidence threshold was too low (borderline content passed)

Identified: A3 (filter disabled), C3 (low confidence threshold)
Root cause: Missing re-enablement gate in performance testing workflow +
            confidence threshold not tuned to production content distribution
Evidence Collection for Safety FTA
Technique 4: Change Analysis (Any Incident with a Recent Deploy)

The majority of production incidents occur within hours of a change. Change analysis identifies every change deployed in the 48-hour window before the incident and correlates them with the observed failure. For AI systems, 'changes' include more than code:
Change type
How to detect
Common failure mode
Code deploy
Git SHA diff; deployment log
Regression in inference logic, new retry behaviour causing amplification
Model version update
Model version field in inference logs
Silent behaviour change from provider; prompt no longer effective for new checkpoint
Prompt template change
Prompt template version in git
Edge cases not covered by new few-shot examples; system prompt truncation
RAG corpus update
Vector store update timestamp; embedding model version
Stale or incorrect documents returned; embedding distribution shift
Infrastructure config change
Helm diff; Terraform diff; k8s audit log
Memory/CPU limits, timeout values, replica counts, network policies
Dependency version bump
requirements.txt / package.json diff
LangChain or SDK version changes behaviour; tokeniser changes token counts
Training data or fine-tune update
Model card or fine-tune job log
New fine-tune overfits on narrow distribution; catastrophic forgetting
Automated Change Correlation Query
Technique 5: Comparative Analysis (Quality Degradation)
When quality degrades gradually rather than failing abruptly, comparative analysis measures the
distribution shift
between the baseline period and the incident period. This is the primary technique for hallucination spikes, tone degradation, and output length anomalies.

import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

def compare_quality_distributions(
    baseline_scores: list[float],
    incident_scores: list[float],
    metric_name: str,
) -> dict:
    """
    Compare quality metric distributions between baseline and incident period.
    Returns statistical evidence for the RCA.
    """
    baseline = np.array(baseline_scores)
    incident = np.array(incident_scores)

    # Kolmogorov-Smirnov test: are these from the same distribution?
    ks_stat, ks_p = stats.ks_2samp(baseline, incident)

    return {
        "metric": metric_name,
        "baseline_mean": float(baseline.mean()),
        "incident_mean": float(incident.mean()),
        "mean_shift_pct": float((incident.mean() - baseline.mean()) / baseline.mean() * 100),
        "baseline_p95": float(np.percentile(baseline, 95)),
        "incident_p95": float(np.percentile(incident, 95)),
        "ks_statistic": float(ks_stat),
        "ks_p_value": float(ks_p),
        "distribution_shift_confirmed": ks_p < 0.01,  # p < 0.01 = significant shift
        "sample_sizes": {"baseline": len(baseline), "incident": len(incident)},
    }

# Usage in RCA:
result = compare_quality_distributions(
    baseline_scores=fetch_hallucination_scores("2024-04-01", "2024-04-02"),
    incident_scores=fetch_hallucination_scores("2024-04-02", "2024-04-02 06:00"),
    metric_name="hallucination_rate",
)
# result["mean_shift_pct"] = +220% confirms significant quality degradation
# result["distribution_shift_confirmed"] = True confirms this is not noise
Choosing the Right Technique
Incident type
Primary technique
When to add a second technique
Latency / availability (discrete failure)
5 Whys
Add change analysis if a recent deploy is suspected
Output quality degradation (gradual)
Fishbone + comparative analysis
Add 5 Whys once the dominant factor is identified
Safety / policy violation
Fault Tree Analysis
Add change analysis to identify what changed that opened the gap
RAG retrieval failure
5 Whys + retrieval trace
Add change analysis if vector store or embedding model was updated
Cost / quota runaway
5 Whys + token audit
Add change analysis if a new feature or prompt was deployed
Provider dependency failure
Dependency map + fallback audit
Rarely needs a second technique — provider caused it; focus on our resilience gap
Documenting RCA in the Postmortem
The RCA section of the postmortem must distinguish between root causes and contributing factors. Not everything in the fishbone or fault tree is equally important:
Root cause
— the condition without which the incident would not have occurred, OR the condition whose elimination most reliably prevents recurrence
Contributing factor
— a condition that made the incident worse or harder to detect but was not strictly necessary for it to occur
Proximate cause
— the most immediate technical event (OOMKill, model version change) — this is usually the starting point of the RCA, not the conclusion
Every
root cause
must map to at least one
action item
in the postmortem. An RCA that identifies a root cause but produces no action item has no operational value.
Summary
AI incidents require adapted RCA techniques because model behaviour is non-deterministic and quality failures are often distributional rather than discrete
5 Whys works well for availability and performance incidents — trace the causal chain to the systemic gap, not the proximate cause
Fishbone analysis is better for quality degradation — map factors across model, prompt, data, infrastructure, evaluation, and external dimensions simultaneously
Fault Tree Analysis is the right tool for safety incidents — enumerate all defence layers that had to fail simultaneously and identify which ones actually failed
Change analysis correlates every change in the 48-hour pre-incident window with the observed failure — for AI systems this includes code, model version, prompt, RAG corpus, and infra config
Comparative analysis uses statistical distribution comparison (KS test) to confirm and quantify gradual quality shifts
Every root cause in the postmortem must map to at least one specific, owned action item — an RCA without actions produces no learning
